entities.name: Rephaim

attributes.description: Spiritual dead; irredeemably evil; associated with the dead and spiritual alienation from God

entities.raw_quote: "the path of the woman... inclineth to the Rephaim" and... "the man that wandereth out of the way of understanding shall remain in the congregation of the Rephaim."

entities.name: Nephilim

attributes.origin: Sons of God + daughters of men (Gen. 6)
attributes.role: Progenitors of giant races; source of unholy spirit communication

entities.raw_quote: "the intercourse of 'the sons of God' with the women of Canaan... account for their gigantic stature and strength"

entities.name: Oannes

attributes.identity: Greek form of Nahash; Chaldean daemon; possibly Cush or Nimrod’s father
attributes.role: First teacher of Pagan religion; associated with Dagon

entities.raw_quote: "Oannes... was probably Nimrod's own father, Cush... Oannes and Dagon... were worshipped as the daemons who first taught mankind the principles of the Pagan religion."

entities.name: Dagon

attributes.identity: Same as Oannes; daemon god; husband of Derketo
attributes.role: Worshipped as a daemon who taught Pagan religion

entities.raw_quote: "Dagon... was the same as Oannes... daemons who first taught mankind the principles of the Pagan religion."

entities.name: Derketo

attributes.identity: Feminine form of Dagon; mother or ancestor of Semiramis

entities.raw_quote: "Derketo being the feminine form of Dagon."

entities.name: Semiramis

attributes.identity: Wife of Nimrod; possibly daughter of a Nephilim-born woman or Nephilim + human union
attributes.role: Central figure in post-diluvian demon-worshipping tradition

entities.raw_quote: "Semiramis... was said to be the daughter of the goddess 'Derketo'... and therefore regarded as a goddess or daughter of the gods."

entities.name: Nimrod

attributes.identity: Called Nimrod the giant (Septuagint); Nephilim-descended; husband of Semiramis
attributes.role: Most powerful Nephilim; god of Babylon; identified with Osiris, Orion, Assyrian Hercules

entities.raw_quote: "Nimrod is called in the Septuagint 'Nimrod the giant'... Nimrod was himself a giant—i.e., one of those beings which were the result of these Nephilim marriages, and in all probability the most powerful of them all."

entities.name: Naamah

attributes.identity: Eighth-generation descendant of Cain; wife possibly of Ham; mother (or ancestor) of Cush and Canaan

entities.raw_quote: "Naamah... became the wife of some person of importance... may have invited or submitted to Nephilim intercourse."

entities.name: Obi worship

attributes.linguistic_link: Cognate with Hebrew 'Ob'—Hebrew word for demons

entities.raw_quote: "Obi,' or demon, worship... is most fully established; and the word 'Obi' is clearly cognate with 'Ob,' the Hebrew word for the demons, with whom the magicians and necromancers of Canaan had communication."

entities.name: Incubus and Succubus

attributes.role: Daemons who engage in spirit marriages during sleep

entities.raw_quote: "They were called 'Incubus and Succubus,' or 'The Lilith.'"

entities.name: Lilith

attributes.role: Female demon associated with spirit unions during sleep

entities.raw_quote: "They were called 'Incubus and Succubus,' or 'The Lilith.'"

entities.name: Hypsuranius

attributes.etymology: Greek: 'issue from above' (i.e., descended from the gods)
attributes.role: Nephilim-descended giant; son of daughters of Phos/Pur/Phlox by Nephilim fathers

entities.raw_quote: "Hypsuranius... clear[ly] refer[s] to the same person... 'issue from above' (i.e., descended from the gods)..."

entities.name: Chrysor (Hephaestus)

attributes.role: Magician and sorcerer skilled in words, charms, divinations; Nephilim-descended
attributes.significance: Instrument for establishing general spirit intercourse

entities.raw_quote: "Chrysor... exercised himself in words and charms and divinations, wherefore he was worshipped after his death as a god."

entities.name: Agroueros (Agrus)

attributes.identity: Identified with Noah; husbandman; father of Titans
attributes.role: Deified patriarch; 'greatest of the gods' at Byblos

entities.raw_quote: "Agroueros... belongs to the tenth generation from Protogonus, or Adam... Agroueros... is called 'the greatest of the gods.'"

entities.name: Titans

attributes.identity: Descendants of Agroueros/Noah; includes Saturn (Ham), Japetus (Japheth), Typhon (Shem)

entities.raw_quote: "Titans... means 'earth-born,' and was probably given to them as being unaffected by the Nephilim intercourse, and in contradistinction to those who were, whom the Pagans regarded as 'heaven-born.'"

entities.name: Taautus (Thoth/Hermes)

attributes.role: Inventor of writing; god of wisdom in Egyptian tradition

entities.raw_quote: "Taautus who invented the writing of the first letters. The Egyptians call him Thoor... and the Greeks Hermes."

summary: This chunk explores the origins of spirit communication and necromantic traditions through the lens of Nephilim–human unions, linking them to biblical giants (Rephaim), ancient Near Eastern daemon worship (Oannes/Dagon), and post-diluvian mytho-historical figures like Semiramis and Nimrod. It argues that these unions gave rise to demonically influenced races and established spirit marriages, necromancy, and ancestor veneration as core features of Pagan religion—especially in Cushite/Nimrodic traditions—and traces their transmission through figures like Naamah, Chrysor (Hephaestus), and Agroueros (Noah). The text explicitly connects these themes to medieval European necromantic traditions by referencing the continuity of spirit intercourse practices into modern times.
key_passages: "the intercourse of 'the sons of God' with the women of Canaan... account for their gigantic stature and strength, and the existence of whole races of such giants cannot be explained on any other grounds."

"Among no races do we find such habitual and fiendish cruelty as amongst the Obi worshippers of Africa... the word 'Obi' is clearly cognate with 'Ob,' the Hebrew word for the demons, with whom the magicians and necromancers of Canaan had communication."

"Chrysor... exercised himself in words and charms and divinations, wherefore he was worshipped after his death as a god. This implies that Chrysor... was a magician and sorcerer skilled in the arts of invoking the spirits."
"Agroueros... is called 'the greatest of the gods'... Noah... became the first or father of the gods, and hence is sometimes identified with Saturn."
"These begat, by connection with their own mothers... the women of those times without shame having intercourse with any men whom they might chance to meet."
